questions.stem: Given $\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \to \text{CO}_2$ with $\Delta H = -393.5\ \text{kJ}$ and $\text{CO} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \to \text{CO}_2$ with $\Delta H = -283.0\ \text{kJ}$, what is $\Delta H$ for $\text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \to \text{CO}$?
questions.explanation: By Hess's law, take the first reaction and subtract the second (reverse it and add): $\Delta H = -393.5 - (-283.0) = -110.5\ \text{kJ}$. The target equals (form $\text{CO}_2$ from carbon) minus (form $\text{CO}_2$ from $\text{CO}$). The option $-676.5$ adds the two instead of subtracting, $+110.5$ has the wrong sign, and $-283.0$ is just the second value.
questions.source: in the style of Atkins, Physical Chemistry

questions.stem: Using standard enthalpies of formation $\Delta H_f^\circ$: $\text{CO}_2 = -393.5$, $\text{H}_2\text{O(l)} = -285.8$, $\text{CH}_4 = -74.8\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$, what is $\Delta H^\circ$ for $\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \to \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O(l)}$?
questions.explanation: Use $\Delta H^\circ = \sum \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{products}) - \sum \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{reactants})$. Products: $-393.5 + 2(-285.8) = -965.1$. Reactants: $-74.8 + 0$ (oxygen is an element). So $\Delta H^\circ = -965.1 - (-74.8) = -890.3\ \text{kJ}$. The option $-604.5$ uses only one water, $-965.1$ forgets to subtract the methane term, and $+890.3$ has the wrong sign.
questions.source: in the style of Atkins, Physical Chemistry

questions.stem: The standard enthalpy of formation of ammonia is $\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{NH}_3) = -46.1\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$. What is $\Delta H^\circ$ for $\text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \to 2\text{NH}_3$?
questions.explanation: Both $\text{N}_2$ and $\text{H}_2$ are elements with $\Delta H_f^\circ = 0$, so $\Delta H^\circ = 2 \times \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{NH}_3) = 2(-46.1) = -92.2\ \text{kJ}$. The option $-46.1$ forgets the coefficient of two, $+92.2$ has the wrong sign, and $-138.3$ uses a coefficient of three.
questions.source: in the style of Atkins, Physical Chemistry

questions.stem: Using bond enthalpies $\text{H-H} = 436$, $\text{Cl-Cl} = 243$, $\text{H-Cl} = 431\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$, estimate $\Delta H$ for $\text{H}_2 + \text{Cl}_2 \to 2\text{HCl}$.
questions.explanation: Use $\Delta H = \sum(\text{bonds broken}) - \sum(\text{bonds formed})$. Broken: $436 + 243 = 679$. Formed: $2 \times 431 = 862$. So $\Delta H = 679 - 862 = -183\ \text{kJ}$. The option $+183$ reverses the subtraction, $-431$ counts only one $\text{H-Cl}$ bond, and $+248$ mishandles the signs.
questions.source: in the style of Atkins, Physical Chemistry

questions.stem: How much heat is absorbed when $100\ \text{g}$ of water is heated from $20\,^\circ\text{C}$ to $45\,^\circ\text{C}$? The specific heat of water is $4.18\ \text{J g}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$.
questions.explanation: Use $q = mc\Delta T = (100)(4.18)(45 - 20) = (100)(4.18)(25) = 10450\ \text{J}$. The temperature change is $25\ \text{K}$, the same in kelvin or Celsius for a difference. The option $8360$ uses $\Delta T = 20$, $418$ omits the mass, and $18810$ uses $45$ as the temperature change.
questions.source: in the style of Atkins, Physical Chemistry

questions.stem: The enthalpy of combustion of a fuel is $-1300\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$. How much heat is released when $0.50\ \text{mol}$ is burned?
questions.explanation: Heat released is $|n \times \Delta H_c| = |0.50 \times (-1300)| = 650\ \text{kJ}$. Half a mole releases half the per-mole heat. The option $1300$ ignores the half mole, $2600$ doubles instead of halving, and $325$ quarters the value.
questions.source: in the style of Atkins, Physical Chemistry

questions.stem: A bomb calorimeter has a heat capacity of $5.00\ \text{kJ K}^{-1}$. Burning a sample raises its temperature by $2.50\ \text{K}$. How much heat was released by the reaction?
questions.explanation: The heat absorbed by the calorimeter is $q = C\Delta T = (5.00)(2.50) = 12.5\ \text{kJ}$, which equals the heat released by the reaction. The option $2.00$ divides instead of multiplying, $7.50$ adds the two numbers, and $25.0$ doubles the heat capacity.
questions.source: in the style of Atkins, Physical Chemistry

questions.stem: What is the standard enthalpy of formation of an element in its most stable form, such as $\text{O}_2(\text{g})$ at $298\ \text{K}$?
questions.explanation: By definition the standard enthalpy of formation of any element in its reference (most stable) state is exactly zero. There is no reaction needed to form an element from itself. The nonzero options are enthalpies for water formation, carbon dioxide formation, and the oxygen bond enthalpy respectively, none of which apply to the element's formation enthalpy.
questions.source: in the style of Atkins, Physical Chemistry

questions.stem: Given $2\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \to 2\text{CO}$ with $\Delta H = -221\ \text{kJ}$ and $\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \to \text{CO}_2$ with $\Delta H = -393.5\ \text{kJ}$, find $\Delta H$ for $2\text{CO} + \text{O}_2 \to 2\text{CO}_2$.
questions.explanation: By Hess's law, take twice the second reaction ($2\text{C} + 2\text{O}_2 \to 2\text{CO}_2$, $\Delta H = 2(-393.5) = -787$) and subtract the first ($2\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \to 2\text{CO}$, $\Delta H = -221$): $-787 - (-221) = -566\ \text{kJ}$. The option $-172.5$ mishandles the factor of two, $+566$ has the wrong sign, and $-787$ stops before subtracting the first reaction.
questions.source: in the style of Atkins, Physical Chemistry

questions.stem: If the reaction $\text{A} \to \text{B}$ has $\Delta H = -100\ \text{kJ}$, what is $\Delta H$ for the reverse reaction $\text{B} \to \text{A}$?
questions.explanation: Reversing a reaction reverses the sign of its enthalpy change, so $\Delta H$ for $\text{B} \to \text{A}$ is $+100\ \text{kJ}$. Enthalpy is a state function, so the forward and reverse paths must cancel. The option $-100$ keeps the original sign, $0$ would imply no net change, and $-200$ has no basis.
questions.source: in the style of Atkins, Physical Chemistry

questions.stem: If $\text{A} \to \text{B}$ has $\Delta H = -50\ \text{kJ}$, what is $\Delta H$ for $2\text{A} \to 2\text{B}$?
questions.explanation: Enthalpy is extensive, so doubling the amounts in the equation doubles $\Delta H$: $2 \times (-50) = -100\ \text{kJ}$. The option $-50$ ignores the scaling, $-25$ halves it, and $+100$ has the wrong sign.
questions.source: in the style of Atkins, Physical Chemistry

questions.stem: Using bond enthalpies $\text{C-H} = 413$, $\text{O=O} = 498$, $\text{C=O} = 799$, $\text{O-H} = 463\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$, estimate $\Delta H$ for $\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \to \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$.
questions.explanation: Bonds broken: $4(\text{C-H}) + 2(\text{O=O}) = 4(413) + 2(498) = 1652 + 996 = 2648$. Bonds formed: $2(\text{C=O}) + 4(\text{O-H}) = 2(799) + 4(463) = 1598 + 1852 = 3450$. So $\Delta H = 2648 - 3450 = -802\ \text{kJ}$. The option $+802$ reverses the subtraction, $-1798$ uses only one $\text{C=O}$ and the wrong water count, and $-356$ miscounts the bonds.
questions.source: in the style of Atkins, Physical Chemistry

questions.stem: A reaction releases $20900\ \text{J}$ of heat into $500\ \text{g}$ of water. What is the temperature rise of the water? The specific heat of water is $4.18\ \text{J g}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$.
questions.explanation: From $q = mc\Delta T$, the rise is $\Delta T = q/(mc) = 20900/(500 \times 4.18) = 20900/2090 = 10.0\ \text{K}$. The option $5.00$ doubles the mass, $20.0$ halves the mass, and $2.00$ uses $2500\ \text{g}$.
questions.source: in the style of Atkins, Physical Chemistry

questions.stem: What is the enthalpy of combustion of methane per gram, if $\Delta H_c = -890\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ and $M(\text{CH}_4) = 16.0\ \text{g mol}^{-1}$?
questions.explanation: Divide the molar value by the molar mass: $-890/16.0 = -55.6\ \text{kJ g}^{-1}$. The option $-890$ forgets to divide by the molar mass, $-14.2$ divides by $\text{63}$ in error, and $-111$ uses $M = 8$.
questions.source: in the style of Atkins, Physical Chemistry

questions.stem: The enthalpy of neutralization of a strong acid by a strong base is $-57.3\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$. How much heat is released when $0.25\ \text{mol}$ of water is formed in the neutralization?
questions.explanation: Heat released is $|0.25 \times (-57.3)| = 14.3\ \text{kJ}$. The per-mole value scales with the moles of water formed. The option $57.3$ ignores the amount, $229$ divides instead of multiplying, and $28.7$ uses $0.50\ \text{mol}$.
questions.source: in the style of Atkins, Physical Chemistry

questions.stem: An exothermic reaction has which sign for $\Delta H$, and what happens to the surroundings?
questions.explanation: An exothermic reaction releases heat, so $\Delta H < 0$ and the released heat flows into the surroundings, warming them. The second option describes an endothermic reaction, the third pairs the right sign with the wrong effect, and zero enthalpy describes neither.
questions.source: in the style of Atkins, Physical Chemistry

questions.stem: The standard enthalpy of formation of gaseous water is $\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O, g}) = -241.8\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$. What is $\Delta H^\circ$ for $2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \to 2\text{H}_2\text{O(g)}$?
questions.explanation: Hydrogen and oxygen are elements with zero formation enthalpy, so $\Delta H^\circ = 2 \times (-241.8) = -483.6\ \text{kJ}$. The option $-241.8$ forgets the coefficient of two, $+483.6$ has the wrong sign, and $-120.9$ halves the per-mole value.
questions.source: in the style of Atkins, Physical Chemistry

questions.stem: Hess's law is a direct consequence of which property of enthalpy?
options: Enthalpy always decreases in spontaneous reactions
Enthalpy equals the heat at constant volume
Enthalpy is a state function, so the total change is path independent
questions.explanation: Hess's law works because enthalpy is a state function: the total enthalpy change of a reaction depends only on the initial and final states, so reactions can be added like algebraic equations. The second statement confuses enthalpy with free energy, the third names the constant-volume condition (that is internal energy), and enthalpy is not a conserved quantity in the momentum sense.
questions.source: in the style of Atkins, Physical Chemistry

questions.stem: A reaction transfers $500\ \text{J}$ of heat to a $50\ \text{g}$ block, raising its temperature by $10\ \text{K}$. What is the specific heat capacity of the block?
questions.explanation: From $q = mc\Delta T$, solve $c = q/(m\Delta T) = 500/(50 \times 10) = 500/500 = 1.0\ \text{J g}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$. The option $0.10$ misplaces a factor of ten, $10$ inverts part of the denominator, and $5.0$ omits the temperature change.
questions.source: in the style of Atkins, Physical Chemistry

questions.stem: Which combination correctly classifies a reaction with $\Delta H = +178\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$?
options: Exothermic; heat is released to the surroundings
Endothermic; heat is absorbed from the surroundings
Endothermic; heat is released to the surroundings
questions.explanation: A positive $\Delta H$ means the reaction absorbs heat from the surroundings, so it is endothermic. The remaining options either mislabel the sign as exothermic or pair endothermic with the wrong direction of heat flow.
questions.source: in the style of Atkins, Physical Chemistry

questions.stem: Given $\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \to \text{CO}_2$ with $\Delta H = -393.5\ \text{kJ}$ and $\text{CO} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \to \text{CO}_2$ with $\Delta H = -283.0\ \text{kJ}$.
questions.solution: To build $\text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \to \text{CO}$, keep the first reaction and reverse the second. Reversing flips the sign, so the enthalpies combine as $-393.5 + 283.0 =$ {{1}} $\text{ kJ}$. The negative result shows the formation of carbon monoxide is {{2}}.
questions.explanation: By Hess's law $\Delta H = -393.5 - (-283.0) = -110.5\ \text{kJ}$, and the negative sign marks it exothermic.
questions.source: in the style of Atkins, Physical Chemistry

questions.stem: For $\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \to \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O(l)}$ use $\Delta H_f^\circ$: $\text{CO}_2 = -393.5$, $\text{H}_2\text{O(l)} = -285.8$, $\text{CH}_4 = -74.8\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$.
questions.solution: Apply $\Delta H^\circ = \sum \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{products}) - \sum \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{reactants})$. The product sum is $-393.5 + 2(-285.8) = -965.1$, and the reactant sum is just $-74.8$ because oxygen as an element contributes {{1}}. So $\Delta H^\circ = -965.1 - (-74.8) =$ {{2}} $\text{ kJ}$.
questions.explanation: Oxygen as an element has $\Delta H_f^\circ = 0$, so $\Delta H^\circ = -965.1 + 74.8 = -890.3\ \text{kJ}$.
questions.source: in the style of Atkins, Physical Chemistry

questions.stem: Using bond enthalpies $\text{H-H} = 436$, $\text{Cl-Cl} = 243$, $\text{H-Cl} = 431\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ for $\text{H}_2 + \text{Cl}_2 \to 2\text{HCl}$.
questions.solution: The estimate is bonds broken minus bonds formed. Broken: $436 + 243 = 679$. Formed: $2 \times 431 =$ {{1}}. So $\Delta H = 679 - 862 =$ {{2}} $\text{ kJ}$.
questions.explanation: Two $\text{H-Cl}$ bonds form, giving $862\ \text{kJ}$ released. Then $\Delta H = 679 - 862 = -183\ \text{kJ}$.
questions.source: in the style of Atkins, Physical Chemistry

questions.stem: A sample of water of mass $100\ \text{g}$ is heated from $20\,^\circ\text{C}$ to $45\,^\circ\text{C}$. The specific heat of water is $4.18\ \text{J g}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$.
questions.solution: The temperature change is $\Delta T = 45 - 20 =$ {{1}} $\text{ K}$. The heat absorbed is $q = mc\Delta T = (100)(4.18)(25) =$ {{2}} $\text{ J}$.
questions.source: in the style of Atkins, Physical Chemistry

questions.stem: A bomb calorimeter with heat capacity $5.00\ \text{kJ K}^{-1}$ shows a temperature rise of $2.50\ \text{K}$ when a sample burns.
questions.solution: The heat absorbed by the calorimeter is $q = C\Delta T = (5.00)(2.50) =$ {{1}} $\text{ kJ}$. By energy balance this equals the heat released by the reaction, so the reaction is {{2}}.
questions.explanation: The calorimeter absorbs $C\Delta T = 12.5\ \text{kJ}$, released by the combustion, so the reaction is exothermic.
questions.source: in the style of Atkins, Physical Chemistry

questions.stem: The enthalpy of combustion of methane is $\Delta H_c = -890\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ with $M(\text{CH}_4) = 16.0\ \text{g mol}^{-1}$.
questions.solution: Dividing the molar value by the molar mass gives the energy per gram: $-890/16.0 =$ {{1}} $\text{ kJ g}^{-1}$. Burning $0.50\ \text{mol}$ instead releases $0.50 \times 890 =$ {{2}} $\text{ kJ}$.
questions.explanation: Per gram the value is $-55.6\ \text{kJ g}^{-1}$, and $0.50\ \text{mol}$ releases $0.50 \times 890 = 445\ \text{kJ}$.
questions.source: in the style of Atkins, Physical Chemistry
